diagnosis: The dominant failure mode is proposal quality, not selection: in every trace, Stockfish’s best move was absent from the 3 proposed candidates, and the proposals were heavily biased toward superficial tactical/forcing moves while missing quiet but stronger improving moves. The proposer needs better move-generation discipline: consider the strongest human/engine candidate first, including prophylactic and developmental moves, not just checks/captures/threats.

The dominant failure mode is proposal quality: the best move was never present among the three candidates, so SELECT had no chance to choose it. The proposer over-favors flashy tactical ideas like checks, knight jumps, and attacks on the queen, while missing simple high-value moves such as prophylaxis, pawn pushes, improving the worst-placed piece, and quiet king-safety moves. The prompt needs stronger guidance to search for the objectively best move first, not the most forcing-looking one.

The dominant failure is in PROPOSE: it is too biased toward generic central breaks and tactical-looking moves, so it repeatedly misses strong quiet or positional candidates like pawn moves on the wing, prophylaxis, and improving the worst-placed piece. In many traces the best Stockfish move was not proposed at all, which means the selector often never had the right option to choose from.

The dominant failure mode is that PROPOSE over-concentrates on thematic central breaks and other obvious active moves, while missing quieter engine-best moves that improve a piece, make a prophylactic pawn move, or prepare a stronger plan. In several traces the strongest move was not proposed at all, and the proposals were systematically too one-dimensional rather than candidate-diverse.

The dominant failure is candidate generation bias: PROPOSE repeatedly over-suggests generic central breaks or standard development moves, while Stockfish’s best move is often a different quiet positional move that never appears in the shortlist (e.g. b7b5, c8e6, h7h6, c1e3, a2a4). SELECT is usually choosing among a weak candidate set, so the main fix is to broaden PROPOSE’s search and explicitly force consideration of flank pawn moves, prophylaxis, and the most active piece move even when it is not a central break.

The dominant failure is proposal bias: the PROPOSE module repeatedly over-generates generic central breaks and misses stronger quiet or non-obvious moves that Stockfish prefers, such as piece development, prophylaxis, or king-safety improvements. In several traces the best move was not proposed at all, so SELECT had nothing better to choose from. The fix should broaden PROPOSE’s search for candidate moves and explicitly counter the tendency to default to central pawn breaks.

The main failure mode is proposer bias toward stereotyped central breaks and forcing-looking moves, especially when the best move is a quieter improving move or a flank/positional idea. In several traces the strongest Stockfish move was not proposed at all, so the issue is mainly candidate generation quality rather than selection among candidates.

The dominant failure mode is candidate generation: in most traces the Stockfish-best move was not proposed at all, and the proposer repeatedly over-suggested stereotyped central breaks or tactical probes. The missing moves were often quiet, engine-like improvements such as pawn luft/space gains, piece activation, or prophylaxis, which suggests PROPOSE is still biased toward generic principled-looking moves rather than the true best move.

The dominant failure mode is candidate-generation bias: PROPOSE keeps overproducing stereotyped central pawn breaks and tactical-looking jumps, while the best move is often a quiet improving move such as a rook/queen/knight reposition, prophylactic pawn move, or piece development. In several traces the strongest move was not offered at all, so SELECT could not recover it; this points to PROPOSE as the module to fix.

The dominant failure mode is proposal blindness to strong quiet moves: the best Stockfish move was usually not offered at all, and the proposer repeatedly overproduced principled central breaks or tactical jumps while missing improving moves like rook lifts, piece improvement, prophylaxis, and simple development. In short, PROPOSE is too biased toward center-strikes and forcing-looking ideas, and it needs stronger guidance to search for the best practical move even when it is quiet.

The dominant failure mode is candidate generation, not selection: in most traces Stockfish’s best move was not proposed at all, so SELECT could not choose it. PROPOSE is over-biased toward thematic central breaks and tactical-looking jumps, while missing quieter improving moves like rook/king safety moves, bishop re-repositioning, and flank pawn moves that are actually best.
